No sugar, no escape. JUST DHAMMA
📜Essays 🎧Transcripts 📝Articles 🌿About 🔎Search
NE

Ascetic Vs Aesthetic

· Transcripts · April 28, 2026 ·

Ascetic Vs Aesthetic

(Hillside Hermitage Talk no.364hh - edited transcript) YouTube Talk

Saṁyutta Nikāya 42.1 Caṇḍasutta

Dhamma Discussion

Majjhima Nikāya 21 Kakacūpamasutta (with parallel Madhyamāgama 193)

Saṁyutta Nikāya 42.11 Bhadrakasutta

Majjhima Nikāya 58 Abhayarājakumārasutta

Saṁyutta Nikāya 42.1 Caṇḍasutta

The Headman’s Inquiry

At Sāvatthī.

There, Caṇḍa the headman approached the Blessed One, bowed down, and sat to one side. Seated there, he said to the Blessed One: “What is the cause and condition of why someone here is fierce [angry/passionate], and is designated as ‘fierce’? And what is the cause and condition of why someone here is gentle [mild/peaceful], and is designated as ‘gentle’?” The Cause of Being Fierce “Here, a certain person, rāga is unabandoned. Because of their unabandoned rāga, others provoke them; being provoked by others, they manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘fierce’.

1

Their dosa is unabandoned. Because of their unabandoned dosa, others provoke them; being provoked by others, they manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘fierce’. Their moha is unabandoned. Because of their unabandoned moha, others provoke them; being provoked by others, they manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘fierce’. This is the cause and condition why someone here is fierce, and is designated as

‘fierce’.

The Cause of Being Gentle But here, a certain person, rāga is abandoned. Because of their abandoned rāga, others do not provoke them; even when provoked by others, they do not manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘gentle’. Their dosa is abandoned. Because of their abandoned dosa, others do not provoke them; even when provoked by others, they do not manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘gentle’. Their moha is abandoned. Because of their abandoned moha, others do not provoke them; even when provoked by others, they do not manifest anger. Therefore, they are designated as ‘gentle’. This is the cause and condition of why someone here is gentle, and is designated as

‘gentle’.”

Taking Refuge

When this was said, Caṇḍa the headman said to the Blessed One: “Excellent! Excellent! Just as if one were to set upright what was overturned, reveal what was hidden, point out the path to the lost, or hold up an oil-lamp in the dark so that those with eyes can see forms; in the exact same way, the Blessed One has declared the Dhamma through various methods. I go to the Blessed One for refuge, and to the Dhamma, and to the bhikkhu Sangha. Let the Blessed One remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from today onwards for life.”

2

Dhamma Discussion

Bhikkhu: I like this discourse because from experience, I know that what is commonly considered as a measurement of internal attainment (gentleness) or non-attainment (roughness), is one’s outward expressions or external appearance. Ajahn Nyanamoli: There is another sutta where the Buddha discusses companions in the holy life who are softly spoken, always pleasing, and nice to be around, but of the wrong view. You should avoid them completely. It doesn’t have anything to do with that outward form. The alternative is someone roughly spoken, not easy to be around, and difficult, but of the right view. You should go and be with them, because that is what really matters at the end of the day. Of course, you can have both, or neither. You could be softly spoken and have the right view. But the emphasis should be on whether they hold the right view, as that underlies the behavior of the person. You cannot just use external appearance as a criteria. B: That is the issue. People want to use external things such as clothing, the way of moving the body, or the way of speaking, as an indicator of internal success. Nm: Exactly. Uniform and decorum. That is all fine for social harmony. You do not go and just start telling off a random stranger or being rude. However, if somebody comes to learn the Dhamma from you, they are not a stranger; they want to learn. If you come across as not so pleasing in your expression, and they refuse to hear the Dhamma because of how you come across, that is their great loss. If you read the Vinaya stories and the suttas, the Buddha was far from mild-mannered and softly spoken when monks did something wrong. The names he called them and the comparisons he drew were quite severe. Because of the weight of their actions, it was appropriate. He was not there to pat you on the back. He was there to lead you out of samsara. Do you think that can be done with just some skillful communication so we all feel good? No. Even worldly issues cannot be solved by that alone, because that is not the root of the problem.

3

If your emphasis is on the level of how you communicate and how you come across, rather than why you do so—whether out of greed, aversion, or delusion—you will never solve the problem. Even if everyone stops uttering bad words, if their intentions remain infected with lust, aversion, and delusion, conflict will sooner or later arise because that is the root of conflict. You can learn manners and how to speak properly, and it will cause you less hassle in the world. People will not attack you or be against you necessarily, but there is a limit to that. The problem occurs when you start confusing learning communication skills and how to express yourself without offending anyone as development in the Dhamma. That is completely misguided. People often confuse psychotherapy, which revolves heavily around learning non-violent communication, with the actual practice of the Dhamma. You might actually become better in worldly terms. But no amount of purifying your expression, cleaning up your words, and perfecting your outward shell will bring you any closer to uprooting greed, aversion, and delusion. Not an inch closer. It will probably take you further from it, because you are confusing a worldly practice with the practice of the Dhamma. Consequently, you won’t look for what the actual practice of the Dhamma is. B: You can polish your outward shell all you want, but internally it will still be absolutely filthy. Nm: Yes, the asavas are still there. The Danger of Circumstantial Agreeability The Buddha addressed this in a sutta regarding perfect harmony. A community is not perfectly harmonious just because they learn how to talk to each other or never use rough words. They are perfectly harmonious if they all partake in the same right view. You could then easily conclude that without the right view, even if the entire community is just smiles, happiness, and nice words, that is not harmony. That is circumstantial agreeability. It will break apart at some point because the root for

4

division still exists: greed, aversion, delusion, sensuality, and desires. That is the root of suffering. If you are not free from desires, no amount of padding your expression will change that. It will factually make your life easier, and it is fine to do it for that reason. If you go around and people are constantly upset with you and harassing you because of how you speak, that harassment is a nuisance. You just change the way you speak so they will stop harassing you, allowing you to do your own thing. But when it comes to the Dhamma, there is no room for compromise. People are already entirely leaning away from the Dhamma with their lifestyle and desires. If you refrain from stating what the Dhamma is just because the person asking might get upset, that is quite a demeritorious act. B: Even if it upsets him, you still teach if he is capable of understanding. Nm: Of course, there is a difference between going around preaching and trying to convert everybody to your views. In that case, people have a right to be upset, even if it were the Buddha himself teaching the purest Dhamma. However, if a person comes to ask a question and wants to hear what you have to say, but then gets upset at what you say, they will often claim, “It is not what you said, it is how you said it.” I have heard that many times. One could easily counter, “It is not what you heard, it is how you heard it.” It is just a way of blaming the other person. If you are honest with yourself, you would recognize, “Yes, I am upset.” There is a desire to blame it on how the person said it and how they come across. But is that really why you are upset? What if they said something you agreed with, spoken in the exact same manner? Would you be upset? Most likely not. The real reason you are upset is because you do not like the meaning of what you heard and you do not want to accept it. That is when deeper reflections need to take place: Why am I affected by hearing this? Why do I resist it? Why am I irritated by it? Is it because it goes against my desires or my established views? That reaction of getting upset in and of itself goes against the practice of the Dhamma. Intimacy with Views

5

Take, for example, the sutta where someone was criticizing the nuns. Whether rightly or wrongly does not matter. A monk who was their friend got upset on that account, and that monk was subsequently told off by the Buddha for becoming too close with the nuns. The Buddha goes on to teach how you shouldn’t get upset even if you yourself are tortured, let alone if it is happening to someone else. This does not mean you ignore the situation or pretend not to know whether the criticism is right or wrong. All of that is secondary. The primary question you can ask yourself: “Am I moved by this other view that contradicts my own?” If so, it means you have not abandoned desire in regard to my own views. By refusing to abandon that desire, you are not practicing the Dhamma, even if you claim to be. Relinquish your desires and your acquisitions, and there will be no basis for you being upset. Or the basis for being upset will shrink, depending on how much you give up of these things. It is as straightforward as that. B: This reminds me of the two words: ascetic and aesthetic. There are the ascetics, who give up lust, hatred, and delusion on the level that matters. And then there are the aesthetics, who merely look like they have given up the defilements. Nm: They are giving it up on a level that does not really matter. That is similar to all the extreme self-mortification practices that were completely misguided in terms of mental intention. The Buddha said they are as unbeneficial as engaging in sensual pleasures. You do not even have to go to those extremes to fall into that category. All you need to ask yourself is: Are you prioritizing your conviction because it offers you security, pleasure, and comfort, or are you willing to question it? Are you willing to reflect, allow doubt to arise, and allow yourself to hear things you do not want to hear? If you hear something you dislike and your immediate mindset is, “This is completely wrong,” you must pause. It might be wrong, but you need to understand why this thing that upsets you is wrong. Is it just because it goes against your desires? Or do you factually understand the contradiction in it? And even if you do understand the contradiction, are you relieved that you found one? If so, it means you are still

6

invested in your desires. Why were you threatened in the first place? If you were so sure of where the practice is, you would not have been threatened by an opposing presentation. By not engaging in such reflections and not allowing that scrutiny to arise, you operate on the level of self-mortification—sticking blindly to a conviction and dismissing everything else.

B: Denial.

Nm: Yes, because it boils down to being free from those desires. From the need to find safety in the world, in ownership, in possessions, and in views. If a person is genuinely practicing the Dhamma for those reasons, they will not be deterred by the manner of speaking or the presentation. They are looking at the bigger picture: “I am about to die. I might not have much time left to practice this correctly. Please do not waste my time with poetry and sugarcoating your expression. Just tell me what I need to do.”

Teaching When Asked

Of course, you use common sense either way. That is not to say you must force the hard, bitter truth into everyone’s face even if they do not ask you. That is just foolish. But if you know the person, and they are here to learn and study from you, mincing words does them a disservice, potentially to the detriment of both of you. B: That is not compassionate. Nm: No, it is selfish. You just do not want to be disliked. You prioritize the fear that “this person might dislike me” over actually telling them what you know can benefit them. B: But then you do not really understand the Dhamma, I suppose. Nm: You might, but you are obviously not as established in it. If you prioritize your self image over the Dhamma, it means you have some development to do. The understanding has not settled in. The point is speaking when you are asked. You can equally put people off by telling them what they do not want to hear when they didn’t ask to hear it. That can be equally misguided.

7

Sometimes people get a certain conviction and cannot resist the urge to go and preach to everybody, standing in the town square and shouting because they believe “this is the truth.” Nobody asked them. Even the Buddha himself, who knew the truth in all its clearest facets, had to be asked to teach. On a number of occasions, Ananda demonstrated the skill of recognizing the right time to ask the Lord for a teaching, and only then would the Buddha teach him.

Majjhima Nikāya 21 Kakacūpamasutta (with parallel

Madhyamāgama 193)

Thus have I heard— On one occasion, the Blessed One was dwelling at Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove,

Anāthapiṇḍika’s Park.

At that time, venerable Moḷiyaphagguna was dwelling in overly close association with the nuns. He was associating with them so much that if any bhikkhu spoke disparagingly of those nuns in his presence, he would become angry and displeased, and would even create a legal dispute. [The Āgama parallel describes his reaction more severely: “…he would respond with dosa and paṭigha, displaying hatred and jealousy, even to the point of arguing and fighting.”] Furthermore, if any bhikkhu spoke dispraise of venerable Moḷiyaphagguna in the presence of those nuns, they would become angry and displeased, and would even create a legal dispute. That is how venerable Moḷiyaphagguna was dwelling in association with the nuns. Then a certain bhikkhu approached the Blessed One, paid homage to him, and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, that bhikkhu said: “Venerable sir, venerable Moḷiyaphagguna is dwelling in overly close association with the nuns. He associates with them so much that if any bhikkhu speaks disparagingly

8

of those nuns in his presence, he becomes angry and displeased, and even creates a legal dispute. Moreover, if any bhikkhu speaks dispraise of venerable Moḷiyaphagguna in the presence of those nuns, they become angry and displeased, and even create a legal dispute. That is how venerable Moḷiyaphagguna is dwelling in association with the nuns.” Then the Blessed One addressed a certain bhikkhu: “Come, bhikkhu, address the bhikkhu Moḷiyaphagguna in my name and say: ‘The Teacher calls you, friend

Phagguna.’”

“Yes, venerable sir,” the bhikkhu assented to the Blessed One. He approached venerable Moḷiyaphagguna and said: “The Teacher calls you, friend

Phagguna.”

“Yes, friend,” venerable Moḷiyaphagguna assented to that bhikkhu. He approached the Blessed One, paid homage to him, and sat down to one side. The Blessed One then said to him: “Is it true, Phagguna, that you are dwelling in overly close association with the nuns? Is it true that you associate with them so much that if any bhikkhu speaks dispraise of those nuns in your presence, you become angry and displeased, and even create a legal dispute; and if any bhikkhu speaks dispraise of you in the presence of those nuns, they become angry and displeased, and even create a legal dispute? Are you associating with the nuns in this way?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Phagguna, are you not a young man of a respectable family who has gone forth from the home to the homeless state out of faith?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “It is not appropriate for you, Phagguna, as a young man of a respectable family who has gone forth from the home to the homeless state out of faith, to dwell in overly close association with the nuns. Therefore, even if someone speaks disparagingly of those nuns in your presence, you should abandon any household desires and household vitakka. You should train yourself like this: ‘My citta will remain unaltered, I will utter no evil speech, and I will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion.’ This is how you should train yourself.

9

“Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone were to strike those nuns with their hand, with a clod, with a stick, or with a knife in your presence, you should abandon any household desires and household vitakka. You should train yourself like this: ‘My citta will remain unaltered, I will utter no evil speech, and I will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion.’ This is how you should train yourself. “Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone were to speak disparagingly of you directly to your face, you should abandon any household desires and household vitakka. You should train yourself like this: ‘My citta will remain unaltered, I will utter no evil speech, and I will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion.’ This is how you should train yourself. “Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone were to strike you with their hand, with a clod, with a stick, or with a knife, you should abandon any household desires and household vitakka. You should train yourself like this: ‘My citta will remain unaltered, I will utter no evil speech, and I will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion.’ This is how you should train yourself.” [The Āgama parallel completely replaces this specific progression of hypothetical insults and assaults with a broader, singular psychological instruction: “Phagguna! Since you have left home, gone forth into homelessness, and trained in the path out of utmost faith, you should train accordingly: If there is kāma and vitakka dependent on the household life, sever them. If there is desire and vitakka dependent on freedom from kāma, you should practice that, cultivate it through bhāvanā, and broadly propagate it. Phagguna, you should train yourself in this way.”] Then the Blessed One addressed the assembly: [The Āgama parallel adds that before praising the monks, the Buddha first asks the rest of the assembly if they left home out of faith, and applies the exact same rule to them: “Therefore, since you have done so out of utmost faith, you should train accordingly: If there is kāma and vitakka dependent on the household life, sever them. If there is desire and vitakka dependent on freedom from kāma, you should practice that, cultivate it through bhāvanā, and broadly propagate it. You should all train in this way.”]

10

“There was an occasion when my citta was pleased by the bhikkhus. Here, I addressed the bhikkhus, saying: ‘I eat my meal in a single sitting. By eating in a single sitting, I experience freedom from illness, freedom from sickness, lightness, strength, and a comfortable abiding. Come, you too should eat your meals in a single sitting. By eating in a single sitting, you too will experience freedom from illness, freedom from sickness, lightness, strength, and a comfortable abiding.’ [The Āgama parallel elaborates on the single-sitting meal practice with additional benefits and a caveat regarding socialization: “Whether a bhikkhu has many acquaintances or few, they should all entirely train in eating at a single sitting. Having trained in eating at a single sitting, they become free from activity and seeking; they are free from illness and pain, their kāya is light and agile, their energy is healthy and strong, and they dwell secure in sukha.”] “I had no need to do any instruction for those bhikkhus; I only had to encourage them towards sati. Just as a yoked thoroughbred chariot might stand on even ground at a crossroads with the goad lying ready, and an expert horse-trainer, a driver of horses to be tamed, might mount it, take the reins in his left hand and the goad in his right, and drive it forward and back wherever and however he wishes. In the exact same way, I had no need to do any instruction for those bhikkhus; I only had to encourage them towards sati. [The Āgama parallel explicitly connects the chariot simile to generating sati and navigating the eightfold path: “Because of this, those bhikkhus generated sati towards the dhammā and minor dhammā. It was just like a horse-drawn chariot ridden by a skilled driver holding the reins in his left hand and the whip in his right, traveling along the eightfold path wherever his mano directed.”] “Therefore, you too must abandon the unwholesome and make a devoted application to wholesome qualities. In this way, you too will attain growth, increase, and abundance in this Dhamma and Discipline. Just as there might be a great sal forest near a village or town, overgrown with castor-oil plants, and a man might arise desiring its welfare, benefit, and security from bondage. He would cut down and throw out the crooked, sap-robbing sal saplings and clean up the inside of the forest, while properly tending to the straight and well-grown sal saplings. In this way, over time, that sal forest would attain growth, increase, and abundance. In the exact same

11

way, you too must abandon the unwholesome and make a devoted application to wholesome qualities. In this way, you too will attain growth, increase, and abundance in this Dhamma and Discipline. [The Āgama parallel heavily expands the forest simile into a detailed illustration of agricultural diligence regarding the Dhamma: “They generated sati towards the dhammā and minor dhammā just like a fine piece of land with a Sal tree forest. If the person tending the forest is clever, wise, and not lazy, he tends the roots of the Sal trees at the proper times. He frequently hoes and applies manure, irrigates them with water, digs down the high spots, and fills in the low spots. If bad weeds grow on the edges, he plucks, removes, and discards them. If trees grow crooked, twisted, bad, and unstraight, he uproots them and casts them out. If branches grow horizontally and crookedly, he prunes and tends to them. If the newly grown trees near the edges are regulated, straight, and fine, he tends to them in due season, frequently hoeing, manuring, and irrigating them. In this way, the Sal tree forest on that fine land progressively flourishes and thrives.”] [Before introducing the story of Vedehikā below, the Āgama parallel completely restructures the narrative by taking the teaching on “superficial compliance”—which appears after the story in the Pali—and placing it here, greatly expanding it to contrast compliance for material requisites against compliance for the sake of true withdrawal (viveka). It states: “I do not commend good speech and compliant behavior that is solely for the sake of robes, alms bowls, food and drink, beds and seats, and medicines, and various living requisites. Why is this? If that bhikkhu fails to obtain these things, he reverts to bad speech and non-compliance, thereby accomplishing the dhammā of bad speech and non-compliance. However, if a bhikkhu acts for the sake of viveka, is dependent on viveka, and dwells in viveka, and thereby has good speech and compliance—accomplishing the dhammā of good speech and compliance—I commend his good speech and compliance. Why is this? Perhaps there is one who guards the conduct of the Sugata merely because others do not use evil speech toward him. If others do not use evil speech, he exhibits no dosa and paṭigha, no hatred or jealousy; he is not entangled by domanassa, he does not increase dosa and paṭigha, and he does not expose [reveal] evil. Seeing him, other

12

bhikkhus might form the vitakka: ‘This venerable one has khanti, is enduring and gentle, well-restrained, well-samāhita, and well-pacified in samatha.’ But if others use evil speech toward him, he then exhibits dosa and paṭigha, hatred and jealousy; he becomes entangled by domanassa, and out of hatred and paṭigha, he exposes evil. Seeing this, other bhikkhus will form the vitakka: ‘This venerable one has an evil nature; he is impatient, corrupt, rough, and fierce; he is not samāhita, not restrained, and not pacified in samatha.’ Why is this?”] “Formerly, in this very Sāvatthī, there was a housewife named Vedehikā. A kalyāṇa report had gone around about her like this: ‘Vedehikā the housewife is gentle, Vedehikā the housewife is humble, Vedehikā the housewife is calm.’ [The Āgama parallel emphasizes her vast material wealth as part of her reputation: “She was extremely wealthy and lived in sukha, possessing much money, wealth, and uncountable livestock and properties. She had fiefdoms, tax-estates, abundant rice and grain, and various kinds of living requisites.”] Now, Vedehikā the housewife had a maidservant named Kāḷī, who was capable, not lazy, and well-organized in her work. Then Kāḷī the maidservant thought: ‘A kalyāṇa report has gone around about my lady like this: “Vedehikā the housewife is gentle, humble, and calm.” Does my lady actually have anger existing internally that she does not manifest, or does she not have it? Or is it because my work is so well-organized that she does not manifest the anger existing internally? What if I were to test my lady?’ “Then Kāḷī the maidservant got up late in the day. Vedehikā the housewife said to her: ‘Hey, Kāḷī!’ ‘What is it, lady?’ ‘Why did you get up so late in the day?’ ‘For no reason, lady.’ ‘For no reason, you wicked maid, you get up late in the day!’ Being angry and displeased, she frowned. “Then Kāḷī the maidservant thought: ‘My lady does indeed have anger existing internally that she does not manifest, it is not that she does not have it. It is because my work is so well-organized that she does not manifest the internal anger. What if I were to test my lady to a further measure?’

13

“Then Kāḷī the maidservant got up even later in the day. Vedehikā the housewife said to her: ‘Hey, Kāḷī!’ ‘What is it, lady?’ ‘Why did you get up even later in the day?’ ‘For no reason, lady.’ ‘For no reason, you wicked maid, you get up even later in the day!’ Being angry and displeased, she uttered speech of displeasure. “Then Kāḷī the maidservant thought: ‘My lady does indeed have anger existing internally that she does not manifest. It is because my work is so well-organized that she does not manifest the internal anger. What if I were to test my lady to an even further measure?’ “Then Kāḷī the maidservant got up even later in the day. Vedehikā the housewife said to her: ‘Hey, Kāḷī!’ ‘What is it, lady?’ ‘Why did you get up so late in the day?’ ‘For no reason, lady.’ ‘For no reason, you wicked maid, you get up so late in the day!’ Being angry and displeased, she took a door-pin and hit her on the head with it, splitting her head open. [The Āgama parallel provides a much more dramatic and violent description of Vedehikā’s explosion: “The lady called out, ‘Kālī! Why are you only rising in the afternoon? You don’t do your work yourself, nor do you instruct others to do it. This Kālī does not follow my instructions; this Kālī is showing me arrogance and disrespect.’ Vedehikā then exhibited great dosa and paṭigha, generating hatred and jealousy. The three veins on her forehead bulged out. With a wrinkled, angry face, she went over herself, locked the doors, lowered the bar, and struck Kālī on the head with a large stick, breaking her head and causing blood to flow.”] “Then Kāḷī the maidservant, with a broken head and dripping blood, complained to the neighbors: ‘See, lady, the action of the gentle one! See, lady, the action of the humble one! See, lady, the action of the calm one! How can she take a door-pin and hit her only maidservant’s head, splitting it open, simply out of anger and displeasure because she got up late in the day?’ “Then, after some time, an evil report went up about Vedehikā the housewife like this: ‘Vedehikā the housewife is fierce, Vedehikā the housewife is not humble, Vedehikā the housewife is not calm.’

14

“In the exact same way, here a certain bhikkhu is extremely gentle, extremely humble, and extremely calm only as long as disagreeable pathways of speech do not touch him. But when disagreeable pathways of speech touch the bhikkhu, it is then that the bhikkhu is to be known as ‘gentle,’ ‘humble,’ and ‘calm.’ “I do not say that a bhikkhu is compliant and attains compliance if he is compliant simply for the sake of robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicinal supplies for the sick. Why is that? Because if that bhikkhu does not obtain robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicinal supplies for the sick, he is not compliant and does not attain compliance. But whichever bhikkhu is compliant and attains compliance by entirely revering, respecting, honoring, venerating, and esteeming the Dhamma, him I call compliant. [As noted above, the Āgama parallel places this entire lesson on compliance before the Vedehikā story, using the narrative as the concluding proof of the doctrine.] Therefore, you should train yourselves like this: ‘We will be compliant and attain compliance by entirely revering, respecting, honoring, venerating, and esteeming the Dhamma.’ This is how you should train yourselves. “There are these five pathways of speech by which others might speak to you: timely or untimely; true or untrue; gentle or harsh; connected with welfare or connected with non-welfare; with a mettā-citta or with inner aversion. Others might speak to you timely or untimely; true or untrue; gentle or harsh; connected with welfare or connected with non-welfare; with a mettā-citta or with inner aversion. Therein also you should train yourselves like this: ‘Our citta will remain unaltered, we will utter no evil speech, and we will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion. And we will dwell pervading that person with a citta accompanied by mettā, and having that as an object, we will dwell pervading the entire world with a citta accompanied by mettā, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. [The Āgama parallel explicitly expands the instruction here from just pervading mettā to deliberately pervading all four Brahmavihāras: “My citta, accompanied by mettā, will pervade and fill one direction, accomplishing this and dwelling in it. In

15

this way, the second, third, and fourth directions, the four intermediate directions, as well as above and below, universally encompassing all—my citta, accompanied by mettā, without knots, without enmity, without paṭigha, and without argument, extremely broad, immensely great, measureless, and well-developed, will pervade and fill the entire world, accomplishing this and dwelling in it.’ Similarly, you should train your citta to be accompanied by karuṇā, muditā, and upekkhā, pervading and filling the entire world.”] “Just as a man might come bringing a hoe and basket, and say: ‘I will make this great earth non-earth.’ He would dig here and there, scatter dirt here and there, spit here and there, and urinate here and there, saying: ‘You are non-earth, you are non-earth.’ What do you think, would that man make this great earth non-earth?” “No, venerable sir.” “Why is that? Because this great earth is deep and immeasurable. It is not easy to make it non-earth; that man would only become a partaker of weariness and vexation. In the exact same way, there are these five pathways of speech… Therein also you should train yourselves like this: ‘Our citta will remain unaltered, we will utter no evil speech, and we will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion. And we will dwell pervading that person with a citta accompanied by mettā, and having that as an object, we will dwell pervading the entire world with a citta similar to the earth, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. “Just as a man might come bringing lac, turmeric, indigo, or madder, and say: ‘I will paint a picture in this space, I will make the manifestation of a form.’ What do you think, would that man paint a picture in this space and make the manifestation of a form?” “No, venerable sir.” “Why is that? Because this space is formless and invisible. It is not easy to paint a picture and make the manifestation of a form there; that man would only become a partaker of weariness and vexation. In the exact same way, there are these five pathways of speech… you should train yourselves like this: ‘…we will dwell pervading

16

the entire world with a citta similar to space, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. “Just as a man might come bringing a blazing grass torch, and say: ‘I will heat up and completely heat up the river Ganges with this blazing grass torch.’ What do you think, would that man heat up and completely heat up the river Ganges with that blazing grass torch?” “No, venerable sir.” “Why is that? Because the river Ganges is deep and immeasurable. It is not easy to heat it up and completely heat it up with a blazing grass torch; that man would only become a partaker of weariness and vexation. In the exact same way, there are these five pathways of speech… you should train yourselves like this: ‘…we will dwell pervading the entire world with a citta similar to the Ganges, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. “Just as there might be a catskin-bag that has been rubbed, well-rubbed, completely well-rubbed, made soft and downy, with its rustling and crackling cut off. Then a man might come bringing a stick or a potsherd, and say: ‘I will make this catskin-bag rustle and crackle with this stick or potsherd.’ What do you think, would that man make it rustle and crackle with a stick or potsherd?” [The Āgama parallel details a much more varied assault on the catskin-bag: “If someone were to punch it with their fists, throw stones at it, beat it with a stick, chop at it with a knife, or dash it against the ground…”] “No, venerable sir.” “Why is that? Because that catskin-bag has been completely well-rubbed and made soft, with its rustling and crackling cut off. It is not easy to make it rustle and crackle with a stick or potsherd; that man would only become a partaker of weariness and vexation. In the exact same way, there are these five pathways of speech by which others might speak to you… Therein also you should train yourselves like this: ‘Our citta will remain unaltered, we will utter no evil speech, and we will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion. And we will

17

dwell pervading that person with a citta accompanied by mettā, and having that as an object, we will dwell pervading the entire world with a citta similar to a catskin-bag, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. “Even if bandits were to cut you limb from limb with a double-handled saw, whoever should corrupt their mano in that situation would not be a doer of my teaching. Therein also you should train yourselves like this: ‘Our citta will remain unaltered, we will utter no evil speech, and we will dwell sympathetic for their welfare, with a mettā-citta, without inner aversion. And we will dwell pervading that person with a citta accompanied by mettā, and having that as an object, we will dwell pervading the entire world with a citta accompanied by mettā, abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, and without ill will.’ This is how you should train yourselves. [The Āgama parallel again specifies extending all four Brahmavihāras in the face of this torture: “My citta, accompanied by mettā, karuṇā, muditā, and upekkhā… will pervade and fill the entire world.”] “If you should frequently bring this saw-simile advice to mano. Do you see any pathway of speech, subtle or coarse, that you would not consent to?” “No, venerable sir.” “Therefore, frequently bring this saw-simile advice to mano. That will be for your welfare and sukha for a long night.” [The Āgama parallel greatly expands the concluding promises of the discourse, guaranteeing safety in all directions, the perpetual growth of wholesome qualities, and the attainment of awakened fruits: “Excellent! Excellent! You should frequently maintain sati of this samaṇa teaching of the simile of the sharp saw. If you travel to the eastern direction, you will inevitably attain security and sukha, free from a multitude of dukkha and afflictions. The same applies if you travel south, west, or north. Excellent! Excellent!… Having frequently maintained sati of this samaṇa teaching, I do not even say that your wholesome dhammā will merely remain stationary, much less speak of their decline and retreat. Instead, you will only increase and grow in

18

wholesome dhammā day and night without declining. Excellent! Excellent! You should frequently maintain sati of this samaṇa teaching of the simile of the sharp saw. Having done so, you will inevitably attain one of two fruits: either ultimate knowledge in this present world, or, if there is a remainder, the fruit of the Anāgāmi.”] This is what the Blessed One said. Pleased, those bhikkhus rejoiced in the Blessed One’s speaking.

Saṁyutta Nikāya 42.11 Bhadrakasutta

On one occasion, the Blessed One was dwelling among the Mallans in a town named Uruvelakappa. Then Bhadraka the headman approached the Blessed One, bowed, and sat down to one side. Seated there, he said: “It would be good if the Blessed One would teach me the appearance and disappearance of dukkha.” “If I were to teach you the appearance and disappearance of dukkha based on the past, saying, ‘Thus it was in the past,’ you might have doubt and uncertainty about that. If I were to teach you the appearance and disappearance of dukkha based on the future, saying, ‘Thus it will be in the future,’ you might have doubt and uncertainty about that as well. But instead, while I am sitting right here, and you are sitting right there, I will teach you the origin and the passing away of dukkha. Listen to this and keep it well in mano; I will speak.” “Yes,” Bhadraka agreed. The Root of Dukkha in the Present The Blessed One said this: “What do you think? Are there people here in Uruvelakappa whose execution,

19

imprisonment, loss, or censure would cause sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair to arise in you?” “Yes, there are people in Uruvelakappa whose execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure would cause sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair to arise in me.” “But are there people here in Uruvelakappa whose execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure would not cause sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair to arise in you?” “There are such people.” “What is the cause and condition why the execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure of some people in Uruvelakappa would cause you such sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair, while for others it would not?” “For the people whose execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure would cause me sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair, there is chanda and rāga in me towards them. But for those whose execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure would not cause me sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair, there is no chanda and rāga in me towards them.” Inferring the Past and Future “With this dhamma that is seen, known, immediate, attained, and fathomed, you should lead an inference into the past and future: ‘Whatever dukkha arose in the past, all of it is rooted in chanda, sourced in chanda. For chanda is the root of dukkha. And whatever dukkha will arise in the future, all of it is rooted in chanda, sourced in chanda. For chanda is the root of dukkha.’” The Case of the Boy Ciravāsī “It is wonderful and marvelous! How well-spoken this is by the Blessed One: ‘Whatever dukkha arises, all of it is rooted in chanda, sourced in chanda. For chanda is the root of dukkha.’

20

I have a boy named Ciravāsī who lives outside in another residence. I get up early and dispatch a man, saying, ‘Go, my man, and find out about the boy Ciravāsī.’ As long as that man does not return, I experience anxiety, thinking, ‘I hope no affliction has come to the boy Ciravāsī!’” “What do you think? Would the execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure of the boy Ciravāsī cause sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair to arise in you?” “If the boy Ciravāsī were executed, imprisoned, suffered a loss, or censured, it would alter my very life! How could sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair not arise in me?” “By this method too, it should be known: ‘Whatever dukkha arises, all of it is rooted in chanda, sourced in chanda. For chanda is the root of dukkha.’ The Case of Ciravāsī’s Mother “What do you think? Before you had seen or heard of Ciravāsī’s mother, did you have any chanda, rāga, or affection for her?” “No.” “Was it only because you saw or heard of her that chanda, rāga, or affection arose for Ciravāsī’s mother?” “Yes.” “What do you think? Would the execution, imprisonment, loss, or censure of Ciravāsī’s mother cause sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair to arise in you?” “If Ciravāsī’s mother were executed, imprisoned, suffered a loss, or censured, it would alter my very life! How could sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, domanassa, and despair not arise in me?”

21

“By this method too, it should be known: ‘Whatever dukkha arises, all of it is rooted in chanda, sourced in chanda. For chanda is the root of dukkha.’”

Majjhima Nikāya 58 Abhayarājakumārasutta

Thus have I heard— On one occasion, the Blessed One was dwelling in Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.

Then Prince Abhaya went to Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta. After approaching and paying homage, he sat down to one side. Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta said to him: “Come, Prince, refute the doctrine of the samaṇa Gotama. In this way, a good reputation will arise for you, and people will say, ‘Prince Abhaya has refuted the doctrine of the samaṇa Gotama, who is so powerful and majestic!’” “But how can I refute the doctrine of such a powerful and majestic samaṇa?” “Go to the samaṇa Gotama and ask him: ‘Would the Tathāgata speak vācā [speech] that is unendearing and disagreeable to others?’ If he answers, ‘The Tathāgata would speak speech that is unendearing and disagreeable to others,’ you should say: ‘Then what is the difference between you and an ordinary person? For an ordinary person would also speak such speech.’ But if he answers, ‘The Tathāgata would not speak speech that is unendearing and disagreeable to others,’ you should say: ‘Then why did you declare Devadatta to be bound for a state of deprivation, bound for hell, fixed for an eon, and incurable? Your speech made Devadatta angry and anattamano [displeased / lacking an uplifted mano].’ When the samaṇa Gotama is asked this two-pronged question, he will not be able to spit it out or swallow it down. Just as a man with an iron caltrop (a device made of

22

four or more spikes) stuck in his throat can neither spit it out nor swallow it, the samaṇa Gotama will be unable to escape this dilemma.”

The Invitation

“Yes, venerable sir,” Prince Abhaya replied. He rose from his seat, paid homage to Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, circumambulated him, and went to the Blessed One. After paying homage, he sat down to one side. Looking up at the sun, he thought: “It is the wrong time to refute the Blessed One’s doctrine today. I will do it tomorrow in my own home.” He then said to the Blessed One: “May the Blessed One consent to a meal at my home tomorrow, with three others.” The Blessed One consented by his silence. Understanding the Blessed One’s consent, Prince Abhaya rose, paid homage, circumambulated him, and departed. When the night had passed, the Blessed One dressed in the morning, took his bowl and robe, and went to Prince Abhaya’s home, where he sat on the prepared seat. Prince Abhaya personally served the Blessed One with exquisite hard and soft food until he was satisfied. When the Blessed One had finished eating and had withdrawn his hand and bowl, the prince took a lower seat, sat to one side, and asked: “Would the Tathāgata speak speech that is unendearing and disagreeable to others?” “There is no one-sided answer to this, Prince.” “Then the Nigaṇṭhas are destroyed here!” “But why do you say the Nigaṇṭhas are destroyed?” Prince Abhaya Reveals the Trap Prince Abhaya then recounted his entire conversation with Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, explaining how the Nigaṇṭha had instructed him to trap the samaṇa Gotama with a two-pronged question regarding whether he would ever speak unendearing and disagreeable speech, comparing the inescapable dilemma to an iron caltrop stuck in the throat.

The Tathāgata’s Compassion

Now at that time, a tender young boy was lying on his back on Prince Abhaya’s lap. The Blessed One asked him:

23

“What do you think? If this boy, due to your negligence or the nurse’s negligence, put a stick or a sharp object into his mouth, what would you do to him?” “I would pull it out. If I couldn’t pull it out immediately, I would hold his head with my left hand and use a hooked finger of my right hand to extract it, even if it drew blood. Why? Because I have compassion for the boy.” “In exactly the same way: Whenever the Tathāgata knows speech to be unfactual, untrue, and unbeneficial, and it is unendearing and disagreeable to others, he does not speak it. Whenever the Tathāgata knows speech to be factual, true, but unbeneficial, and it is unendearing and disagreeable to others, he does not speak it. But whenever the Tathāgata knows speech to be factual, true, and beneficial, even if it is unendearing and disagreeable to others, he knows the right time to speak that speech. Why? Because the Tathāgata has compassion for all beings.”

The Charioteer Analogy

“When wise nobles, wise brahmins, wise householders, and wise samaṇas have put together a question and approach the Tathāgata to ask it, is it previously parivitakkitaṁ [reflected upon / thought over] by the Blessed One’s cetaso (citta) like this: ‘If they approach and ask me this, I will answer them like that’? Or does the answer occur to the Tathāgata directly on the spot?” “Let me ask you a question in return, and you may answer as you please. Are you skilled in the parts and sub-parts of a chariot?” “Yes, I am skilled in the parts of a chariot.” “If people were to approach you and ask, ‘What is the name of this chariot part?’, is it previously parivitakkitaṁ [reflected upon] by your cetaso: ‘If they approach and ask me this, I will answer them like that’? Or does the answer occur to you directly on the spot?” “Since I am a well-established charioteer who is skilled in chariot parts, all its parts and sub-parts are well-known to me. The answer would occur to me directly on the spot.”

24

“In exactly the same way, when wise nobles, brahmins, householders, and samaṇas have put together a question and ask the Tathāgata, the answer occurs to him directly on the spot. Why? Because the Tathāgata has thoroughly penetrated the dhammadhātu [Dhamma-element / principle of truth], and through the thorough penetration of that dhammadhātu, the answer occurs to him directly on the spot.”

Conclusion

When this was said, Prince Abhaya declared:
“Magnificent! Magnificent! … From this day forward, may the Blessed One remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for as long as life lasts.”

Just Dhamma

A quiet place for study, reflection, and practice.

Essays Transcripts About Search
